title: Microservices Architecture
summary: Decomposition strategies, bounded contexts, service ownership, anti-patterns, and when microservices are the wrong choice.

sections.content:
## What are Microservices?

Microservices are an architectural style where an application is structured as a collection of **small, independently deployable services**, each running in its own process and communicating via lightweight APIs.

Key characteristics:
- **Single responsibility** — each service does one thing well
- **Independent deployment** — deploy one service without touching others
- **Own data store** — no shared databases between services
- **Decentralized governance** — teams choose their own tech stacks

sections.title: Monolith vs Microservices
sections.content:
graph TB
    subgraph Monolith
        M[Single Deployable Unit]
        M --- UI[UI Layer]
        M --- BL[Business Logic]
        M --- DAL[Data Layer]
        M --- DB[(Single Database)]
    end
    subgraph Microservices
        LB[API Gateway]
        LB --> OS[Order Service]
        LB --> US[User Service]
        LB --> PS[Payment Service]
        LB --> NS[Notification Service]
        OS --- ODB[(Orders DB)]
        US --- UDB[(Users DB)]
        PS --- PDB[(Payments DB)]
    end
    style LB fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff

## Decomposition Strategies

### Decompose by Business Capability
Identify the distinct capabilities the business performs and create a service per capability.

```
E-commerce capabilities:
- Product Catalog   → ProductService
- Shopping Cart     → CartService
- Order Management  → OrderService
- Payment           → PaymentService
- Shipping          → ShippingService
- Notifications     → NotificationService
```

### Decompose by Subdomain (DDD)
Use Domain-Driven Design bounded contexts as service boundaries — discussed in detail in the DDD topic.

### Strangler Fig Pattern
Gradually decompose a monolith by routing new features to microservices while the monolith handles legacy features. Over time, the monolith is "strangled" away.

sections.title: Service Decomposition Decision
sections.content:
graph TD
    Start[What should become a service?] --> BC[Business Capability]
    BC --> Q1{Does it change\nindependently?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Does it have\nclear ownership?}
    Q1 -->|No| Merge[Keep together]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Does it scale\ndifferently?}
    Q2 -->|No| Merge
    Q3 -->|Yes| Service[Good service candidate]
    Q3 -->|No| Consider[Consider — may still be worth it]
    style Service fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style Merge fill:#6b7280,color:#fff

## The Database Per Service Rule

Each microservice must own its data and expose it only via its API. No other service should query your database directly.

**Why?**
- Allows independent schema evolution
- Allows choosing the right DB per service (relational, document, cache)
- Prevents tight coupling through shared DB

```
✓ OrderService reads users via UserService API
✗ OrderService queries users table in UserService's DB
```

### Handling Cross-Service Data
- **API calls** — synchronous (REST/gRPC) for real-time data needs
- **Events** — async (Kafka) for data that can be eventually consistent
- **Data replication** — read-only copies for performance (analytics)

## Common Anti-Patterns

### Distributed Monolith
Microservices that must be deployed together because they're tightly coupled. You get the complexity of microservices without the benefits.

### Chatty Services
A single user request triggers 20+ synchronous service-to-service calls. Performance catastrophe.

### Shared Database
Multiple services writing to the same database. Defeats service independence.

### Too Fine-Grained
Services so small they have no autonomy. "NanoServices" — one function per service.

### Microservices from Day One
Starting with microservices before you understand your domain. Build a modular monolith first.

sections.title: Start with a Modular Monolith
sections.content: Build a well-structured monolith with clear module boundaries first. When you genuinely need independent scaling or deployment of a specific module, extract it into a service. This avoids premature distribution and gives you time to understand your domain boundaries.

sections.question: What is a 'Distributed Monolith' anti-pattern?
options: A monolith that is deployed across multiple data centers
Multiple services that are so tightly coupled they must be deployed together, giving the complexity of microservices without the benefits
A microservice that has grown too large and needs to be split
A monolith that uses distributed caching
sections.explanation: A distributed monolith is arguably the worst of both worlds: you have the operational complexity of microservices (network calls, distributed tracing, separate deployments) but services are tightly coupled through shared databases, synchronous chains, or shared libraries — so you can't actually deploy them independently.
